stem: How many elements of the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), where \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \), are nilpotent? (An element \( x \) is nilpotent if \( x^{k} \equiv 0 \pmod{[[nval]]} \) for some positive integer \( k \).)
solution: In \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) an element is nilpotent exactly when it is a multiple of the radical \( pq \) (the product of the distinct primes dividing \( n \)). The multiples of \( [[p]]\cdot[[q]] \) in \( \{0, 1, \dots, [[nval]]-1\} \) number \( \dfrac{[[nval]]}{[[p]]\cdot[[q]]} = [[p]]^{[[a]]-1}[[q]]^{[[b]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the radical \( pq \) itself, not the count of its multiples in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \).
This counts the multiples of \( [[p]] \) alone; nilpotents must be divisible by every prime factor, i.e. by \( [[p]]\cdot[[q]] \).
This counts the multiples of \( [[q]] \) alone; nilpotents must be divisible by \( [[p]]\cdot[[q]] \), not just \( [[q]] \).
This is one more than the correct count.
This is two more than the correct count.
This is three more than the correct count.

stem: How many elements \( e \) of the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), where \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \), are idempotent (satisfy \( e^{2} \equiv e \pmod{[[nval]]} \))?
solution: By the Chinese Remainder Theorem, \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \cong \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]^{[[a]]}} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[q]]^{[[b]]}} \), and each factor \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]^{[[a]]}} \), \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[q]]^{[[b]]}} \) (a local ring) has exactly the two idempotents \( 0, 1 \). So the number of idempotents of the product is \( 2 \times 2 = 4 \), one for each choice of \( (0\text{ or }1) \) in each coordinate.
distractor_rationales: This counts only the two 'obvious' idempotents \( 0 \) and \( 1 \), missing the two nontrivial ones coming from the CRT decomposition.
This is an arbitrary small count that does not match \( 2^{2} \) idempotents from the two-factor CRT decomposition.
This would be the count if there were three independent factors (\( 2^{3}=8 \) minus 2), not two.
This is \( 2^{3} \), the idempotent count for a THREE-factor CRT decomposition; here \( n \) has only two distinct prime factors.
This confuses a prime factor of \( n \) with the count of idempotents.
This confuses the other prime factor of \( n \) with the count of idempotents.

stem: The elements \( 0 \) and \( 1 \) always satisfy \( e^{2} \equiv e \pmod{[[nval]]} \); call these the TRIVIAL idempotents. In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \), where \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \), how many NONTRIVIAL idempotents (elements \( e \ne 0,1 \) with \( e^{2}\equiv e \)) are there?
solution: \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) has \( 2^{2}=4 \) idempotents in total (by CRT over its two distinct prime-power factors), and exactly \( 2 \) of these (\( 0 \) and \( 1 \)) are trivial. So there are \( 4-2=[[answer]] \) nontrivial idempotents.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes a ring with two distinct prime factors has NO nontrivial idempotents; in fact it always has exactly \( 2 \).
This undercounts by one; there are two nontrivial idempotents, arising as a matching pair from the CRT decomposition.
This overcounts by one relative to the correct total of \( 2 \) nontrivial idempotents.
This is the TOTAL idempotent count (including the trivial ones \( 0,1 \)), not just the nontrivial ones.
This computes an unrelated difference of the two prime factors instead of counting idempotents.
This is far too large; it does not account for the fact that idempotents are rare, not almost every element.

stem: In the ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[nval]]} \) with \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \), let \( x = [[xval]] = [[c]]\cdot [[p]] \). What is the nilpotency index of \( x \), i.e. the SMALLEST positive integer \( k \) such that \( x^{k} \equiv 0 \pmod{[[nval]]} \)?
solution: Since \( x = [[c]]\cdot [[p]] \) with \( [[c]] \) not divisible by \( [[p]] \), \( x \) is divisible by \( [[p]] \) exactly once, so \( x^{k} = [[c]]^{k}[[p]]^{k} \) is divisible by \( [[p]]^{[[a]]} \) exactly when \( k \ge [[a]] \). The smallest such \( k \) is \( [[a]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is one less than \( a \); \( x^{a-1} \) still has only \( p^{a-1} \) dividing it, not enough to vanish mod \( p^{a} \).
This is one more than needed; \( k=a \) already suffices, since \( p^{a} \mid p^{a} \).
This is the prime \( p \) itself, confusing the base of the ring with the required exponent.
This is the coefficient \( c \), which has no effect on the nilpotency index since it is coprime to \( p \).
This multiplies \( a \) and \( c \), but \( c \) (being coprime to \( p \)) does not change the required exponent.
This is \( p^{a-1} \), the size of an unrelated quantity, not the nilpotency index.
